posts.name: Ilkka |_ |_| mme
posts.cooked:
I’ve done my fair share of complaining here as far as the recent Beaujolais vintages go. The wines just have not seemed to be what they used to be, with ABV levels ranging between 13.5% and 14.5% and fruit quality veering towards Grenache.

While on average 2019 was no less warm than 2018 in Burgundy the early reports are telling a story of a fresher, less solar vintage. While scanning through a number of new releases I noticed that very often the ABV is 13% rather than 13.5 or 14. This gave me enough confidence to buy a few bottles, of which this the first.

Long story short, this is a bloody fantastic bottle of Gamay and exactly what I was hoping for. One of the most important qualities of a good Bojo for me is a high evaporation rate and here it is through the roof. I have a Foillard and a Desvignes lined up next but regardless of how those perform I think I want to explore Thivin’s range further in this vintage.

  • 2019 Château Thivin Brouilly Reverdon - France, Burgundy, Beaujolais, Brouilly (20.5.2021)
    Very attractive on the nose with bright cherry, cranberry and wet stones. On the palate on the light side yet with plentiful perfectly ripe fruit, great lift and refreshing acidity. Supremely tasty and drinkable with a lip-smacking finish. No fat whatsoever but has a very pleasant mouthfeel. Comes off as serious but then is very approachable still. Markedly savory, nothing here screaming ”solar”.

Posted from CellarTracker

Nice timing! Often your praise is detrimental to people’s bank accounts but I find Thivin’s wines for the most part very cheap, both relatively and absolutely.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: I had the “normal” Côte de Brouilly a couple of weeks ago, and loved it, though I felt that some of the pure “joy” of Beaujolais has been sucked away as the wines become more serious.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
I had the “normal” Côte de Brouilly a couple of weeks ago, and loved it, though I felt that some of the pure “joy” of Beaujolais has been sucked away as the wines become more serious.

Love Thivin. It’s almost as if they can do no wrong, even in the ripe years like 15 and 18 where I may avoid other producers or certain cuvees. I get what David is saying, 2014 is the last real classic in this recent run of vintages. And 2011 before that. Love love those two vintages.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I had the “normal” Côte de Brouilly a couple of weeks ago, and loved it, though I felt that some of the pure “joy” of Beaujolais has been sucked away as the wines become more serious.

Love Thivin. It’s almost as if they can do no wrong, even in the ripe years like 15 and 18 where I may avoid other producers or certain cuvees. I get what David is saying, 2014 is the last real classic in this recent run of vintages. And 2011 before that. Love love those two vintages.

I think 2011 was in the end a bit more along the lines of 2017 and 2019: that’s to say, a sunny vintage that didn’t efface the region’s classic profile.

2016 in style was more in the school of 2014, but rendered uneven by climactic challenges.

The vintages I wish I bought more of were 2010 and 2013 from good producers which are aging superbly and actually often retaining more fruit than riper 2011s. Perhaps that’s because higher acid vintages also stand up better to the region’s often rather crude bottling practices.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Nice timing! Often your praise is detrimental to people’s bank accounts but I find Thivin’s wines for the most part very cheap, both relatively and absolutely.

That’s for sure!

Other very nice 2019s recently tasted in a variety of style include Pauline Passsot (Grosse Pierre), Grégoire Hoppenot, Diochon and Métras (2018s got mousey but 2019 seems very clean out of the gates). Will be spending a few more days tasting down there, including a number of new producers, and the prospect of tasting more 2019s has me enthusiastic about the long days (eight producers and four hours on the road today!).

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Nice timing! Often your praise is detrimental to people’s bank accounts but I find Thivin’s wines for the most part very cheap, both relatively and absolutely.

That’s for sure!

Other very nice 2019s recently tasted in a variety of style include Pauline Passsot (Grosse Pierre), Grégoire Hoppenot, Diochon and Métras (2018s got mousey but 2019 seems very clean out of the gates). Will be spending a few more days tasting down there, including a number of new producers, and the prospect of tasting more 2019s has me enthusiastic about the long days (eight producers and four hours on the road today!).

Wow, I’ve been into Beaujolais for more or less a decade but have not come across any of these names (besides Métras, of course). Thanks, will keep my eyes open for them! I can imagine the tastings are a lot less taxing with this vintage than the previous one

Passot took over in 2018, which was also Hoppenot’s first vintage! So this is new stuff. There is a lot going on in the Beaujolais, including at the farming level: just among those I cited, Passot is going for organic certification; Hoppenot has some certified vines and is planning on being fully certified in 5 years despite having some very steeply sloping sites (his tractor flipped last week); Diochon stopped using herbicides and is planning organic conversion in a few years; and Métras has been working that way for years. It is far from easy to cultivate the soils of sites that are often steep and with goblet pruning that doesn’t lend itself to mechanization, and when you factor in the fact that e.g. a hectoliter of Chiroubles sells in bulk for around 210 EUR (Chambolle-Musigny, for context, is more like 2,222 EUR) it requires some dedication to do.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I think 2011 was in the end a bit more along the lines of 2017 and 2019: that’s to say, a sunny vintage that didn’t efface the region’s classic profile.

Fully agree. I don’t understand the praise 2011 gets every now and then - in many vertical tastings it has come across as very lush and solar compared to the surrounding vintages.

Oh, well, I do like the 2011s, I have to say. But I do think that the Beaujolais has a problem in so far as every time there is a riper, higher-pH vintage, the wines taste great during élevage and just after bottling but then loose their fruit faster than one might have anticipated in bottle. I don’t think this is inherent to the sites of the cépage—I’ve had enough great old Beaujolais, including from very ripe years, to feel quite confident about that—but more to do with bottling practices plus comparatively poor quality corks. If you think about the price per hectoliter for Chiroubles I cited above, it makes sense. When the value of the wine is 2.10 EUR per liter, it makes little sense to spend 100,000 EUR on a state of the art bottling line and 80 cents per cork for very good corks. So most of the regions wines are bottled by mobile bottling lines, which means they are pumped and filtered and experience a thermic shock (I have seen bottling lines where the wine is warmed by being passed through a copper tube heated by a naked flame). And then the corks are not especially good quality. Over the long haul, the lower pH, more marginally ripe vintages tend to stand up to this sort of treatment better than the riper years. At least, that is the conclusion I have been coming to lately. Obviously, producers such as Thivin that belong in the region’s 1% are investing in quite good corks, etc, and hence their wines have a track record of aging gracefully.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
No, that’s for sure. However, the Beaujolais did quite well regarding frost this year at least!

That’s great news. It crazy how almost all corners of France seemed to take a bad hit this spring, so it’s always a relief to hear if some place wasn’t hit that badly.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Oh, well, I do like the 2011s, I have to say. But I do think that the Beaujolais has a problem in so far as every time there is a riper, higher-pH vintage, the wines taste great during élevage and just after bottling but then loose their fruit faster than one might have anticipated in bottle. I don’t think this is inherent to the sites of the cépage—I’ve had enough great old Beaujolais, including from very ripe years, to feel quite confident about that—but more to do with bottling practices plus comparatively poor quality corks. If you think about the price per hectoliter for Chiroubles I cited above, it makes sense. When the value of the wine is 21 cents per liter, it makes little sense to spend 100,000 EUR on a state of the art bottling line and 80 cents per cork for very good corks. So most of the regions wines are bottled by mobile bottling lines, which means they are pumped and filtered and experience a thermic shock (I have seen bottling lines where the wine is warmed by being passed through a copper tube heated by a naked flame). And then the corks are not especially good quality. Over the long haul, the lower pH, more marginally ripe vintages tend to stand up to this sort of treatment better than the riper years. At least, that is the conclusion I have been coming to lately. Obviously, producers such as Thivin that belong in the region’s 1% are investing in quite good corks, etc, and hence their wines have a track record of aging gracefully.

Yes indeed. However, I guess you get to taste the wines throughout the quality range, whereas the Bojo geeks like us tend to concentrate more on the top 1% because nobody hears about the huge majority of the small producers unless they visit the region!

However, my original point was more on how I’ve heard praise on 2011 from people who love fresh, cooler-vintage Bojo, whereas to me it has often come across as quite lush and solar. I’m not saying it’s a bad vintage, since I’ve had some very good 2011s as well. It’s just in vertical tastings I tend to prefer 2010s and 2012s due to their more apparent freshness - and this was already in the early 2010’s, I haven’t been to any Bojo verticals recently, so I have no up-to-date data on how 2011 is performing currently compared to 2010 or 2012!

Funny that I read 2011 so differently, granted though I’m country poke compared to you experts. Not that I rely much on CT notes, but out of curiosity i just checked it out, not seeing many notes describing the wine as solar. Now 2015 to me, wow that was definitely a ripe one, but Thivin seems to pull these vintages off very well. Like you, though, I have not had many 2011s recently, only a couple bottles of Roilette, the Cuvee Tardive and the Griffe du Marquis. Those definitely do not show solar, quite structured and still taut.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Funny that I read 2011 so differently, granted though I’m country poke compared to you experts. Not that I rely much on CT notes, but out of curiosity i just checked it out, not seeing many notes describing the wine as solar. Now 2015 to me, wow that was definitely a ripe one, but Thivin seems to pull these vintages off very well. Like you, though, I have not had many 2011s recently, only a couple bottles of Roilette, the Cuvee Tardive and the Griffe du Marquis. Those definitely do not show solar, quite structured and still taut.

It might have something to do with the producers as well. While some great producers can’t escape the sunniest vintages, a few producers make wines that very rarely show much solar character, no matter how warm the vintage has been. Can’t remember ever having a super-ripe Roilette; JP Brun is another one of my favorites who seems to churn out fresh and crunchy wines, no matter how hot the vintage.

So there are certainly lots of great, fresh 2011 bottles out there. My take on the vintage was on the whole - for example I remember in a JM Burgaud vertical how the 2011 was noticeably ripe and concentrated compared to the 2012 vintages and 2010, reminiscent of the very lush 2009. However, to 2011’s benefit, it had a tremendous tannic structure that went really well with the hefty fruit, so the wine didn’t feel out of balance at all.

posts.name: Michae1 P0wers
posts.cooked:
It might have something to do with the producers as well. While some great producers can’t escape the sunniest vintages, a few producers make wines that very rarely show much solar character, no matter how warm the vintage has been. Can’t remember ever having a super-ripe Roilette; JP Brun is another one of my favorites who seems to churn out fresh and crunchy wines, no matter how hot the vintage.

So there are certainly lots of great, fresh 2011 bottles out there. My take on the vintage was on the whole - for example I remember in a JM Burgaud vertical how the 2011 was noticeably ripe and concentrated compared to the 2012 vintages and 2010, reminiscent of the very lush 2009. However, to 2011’s benefit, it had a tremendous tannic structure that went really well with the hefty fruit, so the wine didn’t feel out of balance at all.

For me the 2018 Roilette normale is way too ripe. Maybe it will age into something interesting, but it lacks freshness and verve and quickly becomes tiring, at least based upon the three times I’ve had it.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
However, my original point was more on how I’ve heard praise on 2011 from people who love fresh, cooler-vintage Bojo, whereas to me it has often come across as quite lush and solar.

Well this reflects to some extent a broader cultural shift in the wine world in the last decade, whereby in many cases the same consumers who didn’t buy e.g. 2007 Côte d’Or reds because they found them insufficiently ripe now eschew e.g. 2018 Côte d’Or reds on the grounds that they are too ripe.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
For me the 2018 Roilette normale is way too ripe. Maybe it will age into something interesting, but it lacks freshness and verve and quickly becomes tiring, at least based upon the three times I’ve had it.

I have not had 2018 Roilettes, but I can believe you - I’ve had probably just one or two Bojo 2018s that have not been too ripe and lacking in freshness. Tons of super-ripe, fat or just blowzy.

Well this reflects to some extent a broader cultural shift in the wine world in the last decade, whereby in many cases the same consumers who didn’t buy e.g. 2007 Côte d’Or reds because they found them insufficiently ripe now eschew e.g. 2018 Côte d’Or reds on the grounds that they are too ripe.

Agreed - although I’d argue it’s partly a cultural shift, partly a stylistic shift.

posts.name: Johan R I C K F O R S
posts.cooked:
I think 2011 was in the end a bit more along the lines of 2017 and 2019: that’s to say, a sunny vintage that didn’t efface the region’s classic profile.

Fully agree. I don’t understand the praise 2011 gets every now and then - in many vertical tastings it has come across as very lush and solar compared to the surrounding vintages.

Oh, well, I do like the 2011s, I have to say. But I do think that the Beaujolais has a problem in so far as every time there is a riper, higher-pH vintage, the wines taste great during élevage and just after bottling but then loose their fruit faster than one might have anticipated in bottle. I don’t think this is inherent to the sites of the cépage—I’ve had enough great old Beaujolais, including from very ripe years, to feel quite confident about that—but more to do with bottling practices plus comparatively poor quality corks. If you think about the price per hectoliter for Chiroubles I cited above, it makes sense. When the value of the wine is 21 cents per liter, it makes little sense to spend 100,000 EUR on a state of the art bottling line and 80 cents per cork for very good corks. So most of the regions wines are bottled by mobile bottling lines, which means they are pumped and filtered and experience a thermic shock (I have seen bottling lines where the wine is warmed by being passed through a copper tube heated by a naked flame). And then the corks are not especially good quality. Over the long haul, the lower pH, more marginally ripe vintages tend to stand up to this sort of treatment better than the riper years. At least, that is the conclusion I have been coming to lately. Obviously, producers such as Thivin that belong in the region’s 1% are investing in quite good corks, etc, and hence their wines have a track record of aging gracefully.

Not that it changes the conclusion much, but 210 EUR per hectoliter is 2 EUR and 10 cents per liter, not 21 cents. And 25,000 EUR per hectoliter Chambolle comes to 250 EUR per liter which seems high for bulk village wine…

For someone not all that familiar with the process, why does the wine need to be warmed up when it is bottled?

At least, that is the conclusion I have been coming to lately. Obviously, producers such as Thivin that belong in the region’s 1% are investing in quite good corks, etc, and hence their wines have a track record of aging gracefully.

Not that it changes the conclusion much, but 210 EUR per hectoliter is 2 EUR and 10 cents per liter, not 21 cents. And 25,000 EUR per hectoliter Chambolle comes to 250 EUR per liter which seems high for bulk village wine…

For someone not all that familiar with the process, why does the wine need to be warmed up when it is bottled?

Evidently I wasn’t fully awake when I posted that, because you are quite right! In the Côte d’Or, wine and fruit is priced by the barrel, and Chambolle has been about 5,000 EUR per barrel, so 2,222 EUR per hectoliter. As you say, it doesn’t change the conclusion much, but thanks for pointing out my error.

As for your question, the wine is warmed up to avoid issues with over-filling and consequent seepage that can arise when bottles are filled with very cold wine.

posts.name: Alan Comroe
posts.cooked: I have only had the “normal” CdB. What are the distinctions between the others?

posts.name: J. D Antonio . j _ @ _ y
posts.cooked:
Some are single vineyards/plots, others are selections of best plots, and often older (sometimes really old) vines. The Thivin website contains lots of good information on each of their labels (note that the Les Septs Vignes is the “normal” Cote-de-Brouilly you reference - thanks to William Kelley for noting the US labelling difference).

In short:

Les Sept Vignes - blend of the following parcels: Clos Bertrand, La Chapelle, Les Griottes, Godefroy, L’Héronde, Henri et Les Fournelles.

Clos Bertrand - self explanatory on the name, at the foot of Mont Brouilly, with a slightly different soil composition (the website mentions pink granite and deeper soils) than some of the other sites.

Les Griottes de Brulhié - single vineyard, southeast-facing, steep slopes and poor soils, partway up the hill

La Chapelle - top of Mont Brouilly, vines averaging 60 years of age, very steep slopes and poor soils

Godefroy - single plot, 80-100 year old vines, producing a dense and powerful wine suitable for long aging (I love this wine - I had a bottle of 2010 while in Lyon a couple years ago, when one could travel, and it was gorgeous. Still a wine that could improve with more cellaring).

Cuvée Zaccharie - a selection of best parcels, 50+ years average age of the vines, aged in barrels the longest (~ 11 months)

I’ve had the Sept Vignes and Godefroy the most, and the structure of the latter is crazy. I couldn’t help myself and opened another bottle of 2019 Godefroy this week. It kept improving and was best on day three (just a silicone stopper and stored in the fridge).

posts.name: S Bauer
posts.cooked:
And this. Especially the 2010’s have been lovely. And while 2012 was a bit uneven, nothing bad to say about it either: fresh, pure, high-acid wines.

That’s interesting. I quite liked the 2012s I bought when I tasted them young, but have been utterly disappointed with how they age. The wines I recently drank are Thibault Liger-Belair 2012 Moulin-à-Vent VV, Chermette 2012 Fleurie Les Garants, Raphael Chopin 2012 Morgon Archambault and Thillardon 2012 Chénas Les Boccardes. All were barely ok to drink, but marked by unripe acidity, rustic tannins and a certain hardness. I don’t think most 2012s will get any better with more time in the bottle. For me, 2012 is the only “down the toilet” Beaujolais vintage of the last ten years.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
And this. Especially the 2010’s have been lovely. And while 2012 was a bit uneven, nothing bad to say about it either: fresh, pure, high-acid wines.

That’s interesting. I quite liked the 2012s I bought when I tasted them young, but have been utterly disappointed with how they age. The wines I recently drank are Thibault Liger-Belair 2012 Moulin-à-Vent VV, Chermette 2012 Fleurie Les Garants, Raphael Chopin 2012 Morgon Archambault and Thillardon 2012 Chénas Les Boccardes. All were barely ok to drink, but marked by unripe acidity, rustic tannins and a certain hardness. I don’t think most 2012s will get any better with more time in the bottle. For me, 2012 is the only “down the toilet” Beaujolais vintage of the last ten years.

That’s very interesting. I haven’t had that many 2012 of late, but consistently enjoyed almost all 2012 vintages I’ve had.

“Unripe acidity” sounds a bit weird to me, since acidity is just acidity. It remains virtually unchanged as wine ages, so it sounds odd that wines that were drinking nicely young would have problems with acidity as the have grown older. As for the tannins, I can’t remember a Beaujolais that would’ve been too tannic for my taste, but I had all too many Bojos that were lacking in tannic structure - I prefer my Gamay with a little bit of bite. Hardness is not my enemy, softness is.

I still have a couple of 2012 Bojos, probably need to check them soon how they have evolved.

posts.name: S Bauer
posts.cooked:
“Unripe acidity” sounds a bit weird to me, since acidity is just acidity. It remains virtually unchanged as wine ages, so it sounds odd that wines that were drinking nicely young would have problems with acidity as the have grown older. As for the tannins, I can’t remember a Beaujolais that would’ve been too tannic for my taste, but I had all too many Bojos that were lacking in tannic structure - I prefer my Gamay with a little bit of bite. Hardness is not my enemy, softness is.

I still have a couple of 2012 Bojos, probably need to check them soon how they have evolved.

Curious how you like the 2012s you have left. Unripe acidity is the kind of acidity you have when you eat an unripe plum or apricot. A bit shriek. The young fruit covered it in the youth of these wines, but the fruit has largely gone by now. Tastes differ, I don’t like full-on fruity, soft and plush Beaujolais either, but the 2012 Beaujolais today taste vaguely like 2004 red Burgundy to me. Metallic, green and marked by uneven ripeness. I just hope the wines just have a bad phase. I just looked and I have 27 bottles of 2012 Beaujolais left.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Curious how you like the 2012s you have left. Unripe acidity is the kind of acidity you have when you eat an unripe plum or apricot. A bit shriek. The young fruit covered it in the youth of these wines, but the fruit has largely gone by now. Tastes differ, I don’t like full-on fruity, soft and plush Beaujolais either, but the 2012 Beaujolais today taste vaguely like 2004 red Burgundy to me. Metallic, green and marked by uneven ripeness. I just hope the wines just have a bad phase. I just looked and I have 27 bottles of 2012 Beaujolais left.

But my point is that there is no unripe or “green” acidity in wines: virtually all acidity in wine is either malic acid, tartaric acid or lactic acid - and in 99% of the red wines there is no malic acid, as all red wines go through MLF. The amount of acidity in unripe red wines is just higher, not “unripe” - the taste and general feeling of unripeness comes fruit flavors and other elements, not from acidity.

Comparison to an unripe plum or apricot is rather poor, since unripe fruits are high in acidity and low in sugar, whereas dry red wines have always very little to no sugar to mask the acidity. If 2012 had atypically high acidity or unbalanced proportions of certain acids (like exceptionally high amount of tartaric acid in relation to malic acid), it would’ve been obvious right from the start, not something that would’ve appeared only 8-9 years after the vintage. I can’t remember a wine which would’ve been lovely for the first 5-6 years of its life and then turned “undrinkably unripe or green” - and I doubt 2012 Beaujolais is such a case either. And if the only “unripe” characteristic in 2012 Bojo is high acidity, I really don’t mind, because I love high-acid wines, including high-acid red wines. That’s also the reason why I normally favor cooler-vintage Bojos over warmer vintages.
